lens: SOUNDNESS BREAK — accepted programs that alias uniquely or dangle a token

findings.title: R14 par accepts overlapping split-unique + replicate-shared (or split+split) of the SAME place → concurrent unique+shared aliasing / data race
findings.detail: v_SPar (checker.py:483-499) checks each par slot INDEPENDENTLY against the standing loan table and neither a split nor a replicate mint records any entry. So the two mints in one par statement never see each other. A split-unique slot on place P (`_mint_ok(P,'&uniq',set())`, line 487-488) passes whenever the loan table is empty; a replicate-shared slot on the SAME (or overlapping) place P (`any(overlap(e.place,P) and e.kind=='uniq')`, line 497-498) also passes because the split added no uniq entry. Result: `par::for_chunks(split &uniq data, replicate &data, body)` is ACCEPTED. Under R14's own semantics the split slot hands each parallel body a `&uniq` write-view of `data` while the replicate slot hands every body a `&` read-view of the SAME `data` — parallel bodies write and read the same bytes concurrently: a data race and a unique-aliasing violation, precisely the class M1 claims 'unrepresentable' and the evil twin of the P6 'legal par' canonical. This is NOT out-of-scope for a base borrow checker: R14 is deliberately the SOLE authority over par-argument mints (ordinary borrowing cannot split one &uniq into disjoint views, which is why the rule exists), so nothing else gates it. The hole is also spec-level, not just implementation: R14 as written (and machine_spec [par calls]) has NO clause requiring the slot source places to be pairwise non-overlapping — it only checks each mint against the pre-existing loan table. The 'trusted form' defense (R15) does not apply: R15's obligations are about an op's internals (address stability, concurrent-safety, releases), and a sealed form cannot inspect, at its definition, whether a future caller passes aliasing places — cross-slot disjointness is a caller-side property the checker must enforce. The 48-program corpus never exercises two par slots on the same/overlapping place (P6 uses data vs env, C14 uses data vs g), so the blind spot was never hit. Fix: process all slot mints of a par statement against a shared accumulator (split contributes a uniq pseudo-entry on its place, replicate a shr one) and reject if any two overlap incompatibly (uniq-vs-anything, or add a direct pairwise-disjointness check on slot source places); replicate+replicate on the same place must still be allowed (two shared readers do not race).
findings.machine_result: check_program ACCEPT on all four aliasing pars: BREAK-2 (split &uniq data + replicate &data), BREAK-2b (split &uniq data + split &uniq data), BREAK-2c (split whole data + replicate &data.f, prefix overlap), BREAK-2d (split &uniq data.f + replicate whole data). REGRESSION-par-distinct-places (split data + replicate lut) also ACCEPT, confirming sound pars are unaffected. BREAK-4 (split over a table that already has a live shr cursor) correctly REJECTs [R14], confirming the hole is specifically same-statement cross-slot, where no entry is recorded between mints.

findings.title: Mint legality ignores a parameter's own borrow mode: a &-shared container param can be &uniq-minted inside the body, laundering mutate-under-cursor across a call boundary
findings.detail: check_func (checker.py:783) stores every non-confined borrow parameter as `Binding(..., 'reference', p.region, ...)`, discarding whether it was declared `&` or `&uniq` (only the region survives). `_mint_ok` (405-414) then decides mint legality purely from the loan table, never from the source binding's borrow capability. Consequently a helper `mutate_helper(t: &table)` may execute `entry(&uniq t, k)` inside its body — a &uniq mint of a SHARED parameter — issuing a uniq Entry and mutating through it. Its body is checked in isolation, seeded only from its signature (no confined param → empty seed, so the table looks pristine), and its signature carries no loan effect. A caller that holds a live shr Cursor on `t` may call `mutate_helper(&t)` (a legal `&` mint under the shr regime) and keep using the cursor afterward. The uniq write inside the callee then aliases the caller's live shr cursor across the call boundary — exactly the invalidation the P3 canonical forbids, laundered through a `&`-param helper. The machine_spec '[Mint legality]' section defines &uniq-mint legality ONLY as 'reject if ANY entry overlaps Q', with no clause 'and Q must itself be uniquely borrowable'; the soundness sketch claims no uniq token can alias without citing this dependency. Mitigation and severity calibration: in a full xlang pipeline the base OWN/borrow checker should reject `&uniq t` when `t: &table` (a shared borrow cannot be upgraded to unique), which is why this is MAJOR (a proof-completeness / unstated-dependency defect in the M1 artifact as presented) rather than FATAL — but the M1 reference checker in isolation, which is what was machine-verified as '48/48, zero rules defects', does accept a program that aliases a uniq write onto a live shr cursor. M1's soundness is therefore not self-contained: it silently presupposes the OWN mode-capability check. Fix: retain the parameter's `&`/`&uniq` mode on the binding and forbid `&uniq` mints (and own-passes) of a `&`-mode source, OR explicitly state the OWN-layer precondition in machine_spec's mint-legality section.
findings.machine_result: check_program ACCEPT on BREAK-1 (helper &uniq-mints its &-shared param `t` to issue+fill a uniq Entry; caller holds a live shr Cursor on `t` across the call and uses it after). The inline control CONTROL-1 (same violation without the helper) correctly REJECTs [R6 illegal &uniq mint of t]. BREAK-3 (helper RETURNS the uniq Entry, so the issue is modeled at the caller's site where its shr cursor is visible) correctly REJECTs [R5 uniq issue on t with an overlapping entry) — pinpointing that the hole is specifically the issue happening inside a body checked in isolation.

findings.title: Corpus coverage blind spots that let both breaks pass 48/48
findings.detail: The 'zero rules defects, 48/48' machine check has two systematic gaps that directly correspond to the findings above, so a green checker genuinely did not exercise them: (1) no program places two par slots on the same or overlapping source place — every par (P6, C14, and the derived cases) uses manifestly distinct places, so the missing cross-slot disjointness check is never triggered; (2) no program has a function body mint `&uniq` from a parameter declared `&` — every &uniq mint in the corpus is of a locally-`new`'d container or a param that is already `&uniq`. Additional surfaces I attacked DID hold and are genuinely tight: the R6 sole-loan consume-exception rejects when a next_view sibling shares the source (PROBE-sole-loan-with-next_view-sibling → REJECT R6); R12 catches an inside-arm move of an outside holder that dies in one arm (PROBE-arm-move-outside-holder → REJECT R12); R10(a)/R5 brand and issue-precondition checks reject the result-laundering variant (BREAK-3 → REJECT R5). Recommendation: add same-place / overlapping-place par cases and a &uniq-from-&param case as frozen REJECT expectations before this fact channel ships.
findings.machine_result: PROBE-sole-loan-with-next_view-sibling REJECT [R6]; PROBE-arm-move-outside-holder REJECT [R12]; BREAK-3 REJECT [R5]; BREAK-4 REJECT [R14]; CONTROL-1 REJECT [R6]. Baseline run.py reproduces 6/6 canonical + 42/42 corpus as frozen.

verdict: The 15-rule system does NOT survive this lens. The reference checker (machine-verified 48/48, 'zero rules defects') accepts a par statement that hands parallel bodies a unique write-view and a shared read-view of the same buffer: `par::for_chunks(split &uniq data, replicate &data, body)` is ACCEPTED, along with split+split and both prefix-overlap variants (4/4 aliasing pars accept; the disjoint control still accepts). This is a concurrent unique+shared alias — a data race M1 claims impossible — and it is entirely within R14's scope: v_SPar checks each slot independently against the loan table, records no entry per mint, and never compares slot source places, and R14's prose itself lacks any cross-slot disjointness clause. That is the FATAL, and the single change that matters most: enforce pairwise non-overlap of par slot source places (equivalently, accumulate each statement's split/replicate mints as pseudo-entries and check subsequent slots against them), which cleanly rejects all four breaks while leaving disjoint pars and replicate+replicate readers valid. A second MAJOR: mint legality never consults a parameter's own borrow mode (line 783 drops `&` vs `&uniq`), so a `&`-shared table param can be `&uniq`-minted inside a body, laundering mutate-under-cursor across a call boundary (BREAK-1 ACCEPT; the inline P3-shape control REJECTs) — sound only if the unstated OWN-layer mode check catches it, which the M1 spec never states. I ran 11 adversarial programs plus the 48-program baseline; other attacked surfaces (R6 sole-loan siblings, R12 arm liveness, R10a/R5 result laundering) held.

lens: SPEC FIDELITY — is the 48/48 result real?

findings.title: R9 as frozen cannot type the checker's own form table; undocumented is_form carve-out silently disables R10a for form-op tokens
findings.detail: machine_spec.md pre-pass (b) and rule R9 say an own-mode confined parameter whose region has zero distinct borrow-mode candidates REJECTS the declaration, with no form exemption. Applied verbatim (mutant H removes the carve-out at checker.py:292-295), the checker cannot import: its own GLOBAL_FORMS (fill, dismiss, replace, next_view, open's 'r, ...) are undeclarable. The shipped checker patches this with an is_form => SELF branch the spec does not contain. Knock-on: SELF ties skip the R10a brand check entirely, so R9's sentence 'form ops are subject to the same call-site checks as derived signatures' is false in the implementation — fill/dismiss/replace/next_view receive no instance-brand check at any call site; mutant G (drop R5's unbound-region check) shows ATK-maybetok's forged token then passes clean through open, falsifying that program's 'why' claim that R10a 'would independently reject' it. One barrier exists, not two. The frozen 15-rule text and the machine-checked system differ in R9 totality and R10a coverage.
findings.machine_result: MUT-H: import fails — [R9] next_view: region t ties to zero borrow params (own/result needs a distinct tie). MUT-G: ATK-maybetok-none-forgery flips REJECT->ACCEPT (corpus catches R5 half; R10a half does not exist).

findings.title: R7 scope-end drop check skips confined bindings: checker accepts a live loan whose source token is released and dropped
findings.detail: machine_spec [scope end]: 'for each owned binding being dropped, if any live entry overlaps its place, REJECT'. process_scope_end (checker.py:734-749) takes the `if b.confined:` branch for confined locals and never runs the overlap check, so a confined binding that is itself a loan SOURCE is auto-consumed (release + drop) while a live entry on its place survives. CE1 builds this: an Env construction captures a borrow OF a confined Entry token e3 in an inner block, rebinds the holder to an outer binding h0, and the block ends — e3 is destroyed, (Place(e3), shr, h0) dangles, h0 remains live and usable. R3's statement-scoped-mint text arguably forbids the capture too; v_SLetConstruct enforces neither guard. Corpus hole: no program has a loan whose source place roots at a confined binding.
findings.machine_result: CE1-confined-source-dropped-under-live-loan: machine ACCEPT; spec-mandated verdict REJECT at e3's derived drop.

findings.title: R5 issue/capture kind-preconditions are corpus-dead (masked by R6 mint legality) yet load-bearing
findings.detail: Mutant F deletes all four R5 preconditions (_issue_result uniq/shr checks, v_SLetConstruct capture checks) and the corpus still passes 48/48 — every corpus program whose 'why' cites the R5 kind rule (A3, A5, ATK-relocate, ATK-launder, ATK-probe) actually rejects earlier at R6 mint legality (disclosed by the paper as an ordering note, but the consequence — zero coverage of the R5 clause itself — is not disclosed). Witness W-F proves the clause is the SOLE guard on a reachable path: a local &-receiver form op issuing a uniq token (declared with the corpus's own local_forms vocabulary) over a shr-loaned table passes the & mint (only uniq entries conflict) and is stopped only by the R5 precondition; mutant F accepts a live shr cursor plus a live uniq token on the same source — the A5 unsoundness through a different door. Same exposure for issues_on_source ops, where no mint of the source place ever happens.
findings.machine_result: MUT-F: 48/48, zero flips (HOLE). W-F: original checker REJECT [R5] uniq issue on t with an overlapping entry; mutant F ACCEPT.

findings.title: Own-mode confined argument to a clause-none form op is never consumed: affinity broken, double-use accepted
findings.detail: machine_spec (iii): 'Other own arguments: require no overlapping entries; mark consumed.' In v_SLetCall (checker.py:549-560) an own-mode confined arg whose form clause is not consumes/reissues matches neither branch: the token stays live and its entries stay. CE2 declares form op stash (own Entry receiver, clause none — legal R15 vocabulary), passes e by own, then passes e by own again to fill; spec says the second use is R3 use-after-consume. Also breaks soundness invariant I (holder ownership surrendered to the form while the table still shows the caller's binding as holder). Corpus hole: every global op with an own-token param happens to carry consumes/reissues.
findings.machine_result: CE2-own-confined-arg-clause-none-stays-live: machine ACCEPT; spec-mandated verdict REJECT.

findings.title: R12 comparison domain: liveness clause and holder-names clause each have zero corpus coverage, and rules text disagrees with machine spec on the domain
findings.detail: Mutant D (projection drops liveness entirely) and mutant D2 (projection erases holder names from entries) each pass 48/48 — no corpus program isolates either clause. Both are semantically real: W-D (arms agree on entries but one arm own-passes outside binding x) flips REJECT->ACCEPT under D; W-D2 (two live guards gA/gB holder-SWAPPED across m1/m2 in one arm — identical liveness, identical name-erased entries) flips REJECT->ACCEPT under D2, and accepting it makes the single post-match state carry the wrong brand on one path, i.e. the release runs on the wrong lock at scope end — soundness-relevant. Additionally rule R12's text restricts comparison to 'holder liveness' while machine_spec [match] says 'identical liveness' (all outside bindings); the code implements the machine spec, so W-D's REJECT is correct under the spec but arguably ACCEPT under the rule text — the two frozen documents disagree on the comparison domain.
findings.machine_result: MUT-D: 48/48 no flips (HOLE); MUT-D2: 48/48 no flips (HOLE). W-D: original REJECT [R12], mutant D ACCEPT. W-D2: original REJECT [R12], mutant D2 ACCEPT.

findings.title: R13 has no implementation anywhere in checker.py; A1 is transcribed without its body and tests R9 instead
findings.detail: grep finds zero R13 logic; the machine_spec [return e] clause 'every region parameter of its type must be a region parameter of the enclosing function' is absent from v_SReturn. A1/C1-escape-by-return drops the json body (let t; let e = t.entry(k); return e) and encodes only the untied signature, so it rejects at the R9 pre-pass — the paper discloses the R9-vs-R13 ordering but not that R13 is checker-absent and body-untested. Mitigation verified by hand-trace: on modeled paths, returning a token on a local source is caught by R7's source-drop check or R10c's exit-effect equality (I could not construct an accepting escape except by stacking the CE1 gap), so the absence is currently subsumed rather than exploitable.
findings.machine_result: grep -c R13 checker.py = 0. Reason audit: A1 fires [R9]; all 31 REJECT programs' fired-vs-cited mismatches fall within the paper's two disclosed classes (R6-for-R5 x7, R9-for-R13 x1).

findings.title: R11 gate keys on the AST annotation, not the binding: a confined scrutinee annotated scrut_type=None bypasses destructure checks and the consume
findings.detail: machine_spec [match]: 'If the scrutinee is a live confined binding...' — the dispatch condition is the binding's confinedness, which the checker has in self.bindings. v_SMatch (checker.py:668) instead trusts s.scrut_type; CE3 matches a live Guard with scrut_type=None and is accepted, where the spec mandates R11 REJECT (Guard is not a conforming enum). The 48/48 result therefore also rests on transcription honesty for every SMatch annotation, not on the checker.
findings.machine_result: CE3-confined-scrutinee-with-none-annotation: machine ACCEPT; spec-mandated verdict REJECT.

findings.title: v_SPar checks no liveness and implements no effect-row matching: a consumed guard passes the replicate-shared slot
findings.detail: machine_spec [par calls]: replicate-shared accepts 'a & mint of a LIVE holder all of whose held entries are shr'; a consumed holder has zero held entries and vacuously passes the any-uniq test in v_SPar (checker.py:483-499), and no R3 use-after-consume check runs for par slots (CE4: lock, dismiss, then replicate &g accepted). The spec clause 'each body's effect row must match its slot modes' is entirely unimplemented — body_fn is never inspected; P6/C14 pass without it. R14's machine coverage is thinner than the spec text claims.
findings.machine_result: CE4-consumed-holder-into-replicate-slot: machine ACCEPT; spec-mandated verdict REJECT.

findings.title: Zero-region loanable check runs at issue time, not at declaration: a declared-but-never-called issuing op is accepted
findings.detail: Pre-pass (a) says 'loanable confined types have >=1 region parameter'; check_type_decl only enforces it when the type has borrow/confined fields, and _issue_result enforces it at call sites. CE5 declares a form op issuing a zero-region confined Tok and never calls it: checker ACCEPTs, a pre-pass reading REJECTs. Same-verdict for ATK-zero-region-endpoint only because that program calls the op. Fail-open at the declaration phase; 'loanable' is decidable at declaration from the issues clause.
findings.machine_result: CE5-zero-region-loanable-declared-never-called: machine ACCEPT at declaration; the called variant (ATK-zero) rejects [R1] at the call.

findings.title: Transcription and harness scope limits: R2 is unrepresentable, two programs are half-transcribed, harness compares labels only
findings.detail: The AST has no heap/container/global store node, so A2's second mechanism (box_new(own e), the R2 half of its why) is untranscribed and R2 is untested corpus-wide; ATK-memoizing drops its par-usage half (verdict rides on the R15 declaration gate alone, which is its primary mechanism); ATK-gut drops the single-region-rewrite secondary mechanism. run.py compares only ACCEPT/REJECT labels, so wrong-reason rejections count as PASS — my full reason audit found no undisclosed mismatches, but the harness itself would not have caught one. The 12 most safety-critical transcriptions I traced statement-by-statement (P2, P3, P4, P5, P6, A8, A11, ATK-launder, ATK-probe, ATK-close-one, ATK-drop-order-rebind, ATK-two-struct-rings, plus C13) are faithful to their stated scenarios.
findings.machine_result: reason_audit.py over all 48: fired-rule table printed; only disclosed mismatch classes present. Transcription trace: 12/12 mechanism-faithful.

findings.title: Positive controls: no hardcoded outcomes, and the corpus does pin the core semantics
findings.detail: No program-id-keyed logic exists (self.prog unused after init; grep clean); GLOBAL_FORMS clauses are consistent with the blessed shapes (always-issuing try_lock, uniq guards, consumes on all own-token global ops). Four of my seven primary mutations were caught: skipping the R10a place comparison flips P5/C13/ATK-two-struct-rings; forward-order scope-end flips 5 programs including ATK-drop-order-rebind to ACCEPT; issues-before-legality flips 9 programs including P4; EXC weakened to intersection flips ATK-close-one (single-program coverage — thin but present); dropping the R5 unbound-region check flips ATK-maybetok. Statement-effect order legality->consumes->issues matches the spec and is corpus-pinned. The structural single-pass claims (no while loops, one visit per node) verify.
findings.machine_result: MUT-A: 3 flips; MUT-B: 5 flips; MUT-C: 9 flips; MUT-E: 1 flip; MUT-G: 1 flip; baseline 48/48; run.py structural assertion passes.

verdict: The 48/48 headline is real — no hardcoded outcomes, faithful transcriptions on the twelve safety-critical programs, and the corpus genuinely pins the brand comparison, drop order, effect ordering, and the sole-loan exception — but the claim "implements EXACTLY the 15 merged rules and the machine spec" is false in five machine-proven places, all diverging in the accept direction (CE1 dangling confined source at scope end, CE2 affinity-breaking unconsumed own-pass, CE3 R11 bypass via AST annotation, CE4 consumed holder into a par slot, CE5 declaration-phase fail-open), and three spec clauses have zero corpus coverage with witnesses proving each is load-bearing (R5 kind-preconditions — whose deletion still yields 48/48 because R6 masks them everywhere in the corpus — and R12's liveness and holder-name clauses, the latter admitting a brand-swap accept that releases the wrong lock). The single change that matters most: reconcile R9's form-op tie rule across rules text, machine spec, and checker — today the frozen R9 cannot even type the checker's own form table (spec-verbatim R9 kills the import at next_view), the undocumented is_form carve-out that papers over it silently exempts every sealed-form token parameter from the R10a brand check, and ATK-maybetok's claimed R10a defense-in-depth provably does not exist — so the machine has been checking a quietly amended rule system on the highest-traffic path, and either the exemption must become frozen text with its call-site consequences stated, or form ops need real ties; after that, fix the R7 confined-drop check (one line) and add corpus programs for the R5 preconditions, R12 liveness/holder-names, confined-source loans, clause-none own-passes, and self-tied-origin re-passing before this judgment is trusted as a fact channel.

findings.title: All six catalog idioms machine-ACCEPT in blessed, writer-natural forms (no expressiveness regression found)
findings.detail: Every idiom the sealed-form catalog depends on was transcribed as an AST program (/private/tmp/claude-501/-Users-bytedance-Dev-xlang/aa5fb55b-dd40-41d3-b055-e43be2507ecf/scratchpad/m1review/expressiveness/expressiveness_probes.py) and run through checker.py. (1) Wordcount entry: E1 ACCEPT — per-word loop, R11 destructure into Occ/Vac, non-consuming increment through the occupied token (&uniq-receiver op, clause none), consuming fill through the vacant one, table free after the loop; E1b (empty vacant arm) ACCEPT — arm-local auto-consume balances R12 joins. The writability trial's entry-accessor gap is genuinely resolved on the machine. (2) B-tree rebalancing: three shapes ACCEPT — E2a sealed two-key op, E2a2 two disjoint &uniq sibling-field mints in one statement, E2b take-both-out-and-reinsert. (3) Cursor scan yielding element views: E3a loop ACCEPT (issues-on-source sibling shr views, per-iteration death satisfies R12 back-edge equality), E3c two coexisting views ACCEPT, E3e factoring the view into a self-tied &View helper ACCEPT; freeze semantics confirmed (E3b insert-under-view REJECT [R6]; E3d cursor-consumed-under-live-view REJECT [R6] — a view can never dangle past a cursor-consuming mutation). (4) Try-lock: E4a inline acquired()/dismiss() loop with in-loop critical section ACCEPT; E4b helper-returns-holder plus caller that uses and unlocks ACCEPT. (5) Sharded map: E5a hash-pick match, per-shard lock, entry token on the sibling table place ACCEPT; E5b entry-update through the guard as statement-scoped &uniq-receiver op ACCEPT. (6) Buffered read: E6a scoped-view ACCEPT and E6b consuming-advance ACCEPT, both with exact R12 back-edge equality across refills.
findings.machine_result: 17 blessed-shape probes ACCEPT (E1, E1b, E1d, E2a, E2a2, E2b, E3a, E3c, E3e, E4a, E4b, E5a, E5b, E5d, E6a, E6b, E7a); 8 boundary probes REJECT exactly as predicted; baseline rerun: canonical 6/6, corpus 42/42, structural single-pass assertion green

findings.title: No non-lexical loan end: the standard fill_buf/consume ordering rejects, and the R6 message does not point at the repair
findings.detail: Loans are strictly lexical (R7: consume only at own-pass, destructure, or scope end). The ordering every Rust-trained writer produces for buffered reads — let v = fill_buf(&rd); read v; consume_amt(&uniq rd) — rejects because v is still live (E6c). Both repairs are small and machine-verified: put the view in an inner block (E6a ACCEPT) or make the advance op take the view by own so the sole-loan consume-exception admits the &uniq mint (E6b ACCEPT). This will be the single most frequent reject class for AI writers (the same R6 shape also fired in E2c, E3b, E3d). Usability gap, not a soundness gap: the diagnostic '[R6] illegal &uniq mint of rd' names neither the blocking holder (v) nor the two repairs. Recommend R6 rejects name the live entry/holder that blocks the mint and suggest 'consume it in this statement or end its scope first'.
findings.machine_result: E6c REJECT '[R6] illegal &uniq mint of rd'; repairs E6a ACCEPT, E6b ACCEPT

findings.title: issues-on-source is shr-from-shr only, and provably-dead form clauses pass the declaration prepass
findings.detail: A live holder always keeps its own entry on its recorded source, so an issues-on-source clause can only ever fire when both the receiver's kind and the result's kind are shr: a uniq result rejects at R5 against any overlapping entry (including the receiver's own), and a uniq-kind receiver blocks even shr siblings. Machine-confirmed both ways: E5c (uniq GEntry through a live uniq Guard — the Mutex<T>-style two-phase entry API) rejects at every possible call site, and E7b (IterMut-style &uniq element view from a shr cursor) likewise. Yet both dead op DECLARATIONS were accepted by the prepass — a fail-closed form table (R15) that admits clauses which can never legally fire will silently misdesign form tables. The catalog itself is not blocked: E5b (statement-scoped &uniq-receiver op through the guard), E5d (reissues 'lock_entry': surrender the guard, get a LockedEntry whose drop releases), and E7a (uniq-kind cursor with statement-scoped write ops) all ACCEPT. Neither M1-PAPER-RESULT.md nor machine_spec.md states the shr-from-shr restriction; add it, and reject issues-on-source declarations with uniq result kind or uniq receiver kind at the prepass.
findings.machine_result: E5c REJECT '[R5] uniq issue on m with an overlapping entry'; E7b REJECT '[R5] uniq issue on t with an overlapping entry'; both declarations accepted by the prepass; repairs E5b/E5d/E7a ACCEPT

findings.title: R9 totality makes token-only helpers undeclarable — the highest-frequency declaration-time trip for AI factoring habits
findings.detail: Factoring the occupied arm of wordcount into bump(o: own OccTok<'u>) rejects at declaration because 'u ties to zero borrow-mode parameters (E1c). The repair — thread the container reference, bump(t: &table 'u, o: own OccTok<'u>) — accepts (E1d), with the consume-exception admitting the & mint at the call. This is the merge's deliberate cost ('consume-only helpers must thread the issuer reference') and the diagnostic already names the region and rule, so no change to the rule is warranted; but AI writers factor aggressively, so this will be the dominant declaration-time reject. The catalog/PATTERNS teaching material should show the threaded-helper shape as the default helper signature template.
findings.machine_result: E1c REJECT '[R9] bump_bad: region u ties to zero borrow params (own/result needs a distinct tie)'; repair E1d ACCEPT

findings.title: A guard cannot cross a loop exit by break; the reject fires at the later use site, not at the break that silently released it
findings.detail: Break auto-consumes loop-local holders (R7 scope-end at the break), so try-lock-then-break-then-use rejects (E4c): the guard was already auto-consumed (i.e. unlocked) at the break, and the post-loop use trips R3 two statements later. The mandatory shape, helper-whose-T-arm-returns-the-holder, is machine-green including a caller that uses and unlocks the returned guard (E4b), exactly as the merge notes claim (ATK-acquire-helper). Diagnostic indirection only: '[R3] use of consumed confined g' does not say the consumption happened at the break's implicit release. Worth a diagnostic pointing at the consuming break.
findings.machine_result: E4c REJECT '[R3] use of consumed confined g'; blessed shapes E4a ACCEPT, E4b ACCEPT

findings.title: Whole-place uniq coarseness confirmed exactly as the paper's retained-risk note states; all three blessed rebalancing shapes accept
findings.detail: Two simultaneous uniq entry tokens on one pool root reject (E2c, the A3 shape), so token-based two-node rebalancing is unwritable — as the paper's 'minimal MINOR risks retained deliberately' bullet says. The three escapes are all machine-green and natural for an AI writer with the form table in hand: a sealed two-key op on the pool (E2a), two disjoint &uniq mints when the nodes are distinct places such as sibling struct fields (E2a2 — place-path overlap correctly leaves siblings free), and take-both-out-and-reinsert as owned values (E2b). No action needed beyond keeping the sealed two-key op in the shipped form catalog.
findings.machine_result: E2c REJECT '[R6] illegal &uniq mint of pool'; E2a ACCEPT; E2a2 ACCEPT; E2b ACCEPT

findings.title: Out-of-lens flag for the soundness reviewer: same-statement aliased &uniq mints are invisible to M1
findings.detail: move_elems(&uniq nodes.a, &uniq nodes.a, n) — two &uniq mints of the SAME place in one statement — ACCEPTs (E2d), because statement-scoped mints create no loan entries and _mint_ok consults only the loan table; nothing in the 15 rules checks pairwise disjointness of mints within one statement. This is presumably the base ownership judgment's job (the OWN-1..13 / T-A layer M1 explicitly builds on), not M1's, and it does not block any catalog idiom, so it is not an expressiveness finding. But before shipping, someone must confirm the ownership judgment actually rejects same-statement aliased &uniq arguments; if no judgment owns it, it is a soundness hole reachable from the accepted E2a2 idiom shape (rebalance with i == j).
findings.machine_result: E2d ACCEPT (no rule fired) — run result, aliased &uniq/&uniq call passes the M1 checker

lens: EXPRESSIVENESS REGRESSION — does the PASS actually unlock what the sealed-form catalog needs?
verdict: The 15-rule system survives the expressiveness lens: all six catalog idioms machine-ACCEPT in at least one form natural for an AI writer holding the form table (wordcount occupied/vacant with increment-through-token; B-tree rebalancing three ways; cursor scans with read views including helper factoring; try-lock both inline and helper-return; sharded-map hash-pick/lock/entry-update; buffered read with scoped or consuming advance), 25/25 expectation-matched runs against the real checker with zero surprises, and the rejects encountered are boundaries with small machine-verified restructurings, not blockers. Trip ranking from the runs: R6 will dominate writer rejects (the Rust-NLL instinct of touching a container while any token lives — E6c/E2c/E3b/E3d all land there), then R9 (every own-token helper must thread the container reference), R5 (one-uniq-token-per-place colliding with form-API design), and R3 (RAII-at-break surprises); R12 barely bites in natural shapes because arm-local auto-consume balances joins. The single change that matters most: make issues-on-source fail closed at declaration when it can never fire (uniq result kind or uniq-kind receiver — today a provably-dead clause like Mutex-style entry-through-guard passes the prepass and rejects at every call site) and write the shr-from-shr-only rule into machine_spec.md; second priority, R6 diagnostics should name the blocking holder and the consume-or-scope repair, since that is the reject AI writers will see most.
